behaviors.description: A plugin whose manifest is missing a required field is rejected before any code from that plugin is executed.
behaviors.justification: The critical failure being prevented is code execution despite invalid manifest. A Unit-level test with mocks would not catch a real bug where the loader calls require() on the plugin even when manifest validation fails. Testing this requires a real (or spied) require mechanism to verify that module loading is genuinely prevented, not just assumed by mocks. Real filesystem with manifest files is needed to test the validation gate correctly.
behaviors.test_description: Create a real plugin directory with a manifest.json missing a required field (e.g., no 'name'). Spy on Node's require mechanism (or use a require hook). Call the loader on this directory. Assert: (1) the manifest validation rejects the plugin, (2) require was never called for that plugin's entry module, (3) no error is thrown (graceful rejection).

behaviors.description: When plugin A declares a dependency on plugin B, plugin B's onLoad hook is called before plugin A's.
behaviors.justification: This behavior tests pure dependency resolution and call-ordering logic. The loader's topological sort is algorithm-internal and does not depend on real filesystem I/O, module execution, or external APIs. All inputs (manifest objects with declared dependencies) and outputs (onLoad hook invocation order) can be mocked or spied without losing test fidelity. A real failure (wrong order) would be caught by a simple call-order assertion on mocked onLoad hooks.
behaviors.test_description: Create mock plugins with manifest objects: PluginA depends on PluginB. Mock onLoad hooks with call-order tracking (e.g., jest.fn or similar). Call the loader with these mocked plugins. Assert that PluginB.onLoad() was called before PluginA.onLoad() by inspecting call order on the mock objects.

behaviors.description: When two plugins each declare a dependency on the other, the loader reports the cycle and loads neither.
behaviors.justification: Cycle detection is graph-traversal logic that operates on manifest dependency declarations alone. No real code loading, filesystem access, or external calls are needed. The algorithm can be fully tested by passing manifest objects with circular dependency chains and verifying that cycle detection runs and prevents both plugins from loading. A mock would not miss this failure because the failure is in the graph logic itself, not in downstream behavior.
behaviors.test_description: Create two mock plugins: PluginA lists PluginB as a dependency, PluginB lists PluginA as a dependency. Call the loader. Assert: (1) the loader detects and reports a cycle, (2) neither onLoad hook is called, (3) the loader does not throw but gracefully reports the cycle.

behaviors.description: A plugin's onLoad hook is only invoked after the plugin's entry module has been imported and executed.
behaviors.justification: The critical requirement is that real module import (require or import) must happen before the hook is called. A Unit-level mock would hide whether the actual require mechanism was invoked. A real failure would be the loader calling onLoad without importing the module entry point. To catch this, the test must verify that Node's require mechanism was actually triggered (or attempted) before the hook. This requires real plugin files or a sophisticated require spy that verifies the sequencing of real imports.
behaviors.test_description: Create a real (or temporary) plugin directory with an entry module that sets a marker (e.g., a global flag or a module-level side effect) when loaded. Spy on the require mechanism or set a marker function in the module. Create a mock onLoad hook. Call the loader. Assert that the entry module was required/executed before the onLoad hook was called by checking execution order of the spy.

behaviors.description: When a plugin throws an error during its onLoad hook, the loader continues initializing remaining plugins that do not depend on it.
behaviors.justification: This tests error handling and conditional plugin loading, both of which are pure logic. The test can mock multiple plugins where one onLoad throws an exception, and verify that other non-dependent plugins still have their onLoad called. No real filesystem, code execution, network, or LLM calls are involved. A mock onLoad that throws is sufficient to trigger the error path and verify that the loader's error recovery logic works correctly.
behaviors.test_description: Create mock plugins: PluginA (no dependencies, onLoad throws), PluginB (no dependencies, onLoad succeeds), PluginC (depends on A, onLoad should not be called). Call the loader. Assert: (1) PluginA.onLoad() throws and is caught, (2) PluginB.onLoad() is still called successfully, (3) PluginC.onLoad() is not called (blocked by A's failure), (4) the loader completes without terminating.
